emerging_technologies.name: Passkeys (FIDO2/WebAuthn Synced Discoverable Credentials)
emerging_technologies.description: Passkeys are FIDO2 discoverable credentials that synchronize across a user's devices through platform credential managers (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, Windows Hello). Unlike device-bound FIDO2 keys, passkeys survive device loss by syncing through the user's cloud account, dramatically improving usability while maintaining phishing resistance. Passkeys use public-key cryptography where the private key never leaves the secure enclave or TPM, and authentication requires local biometric or PIN verification. The relying party stores only the public key, eliminating the risk of server-side credential theft. Passkeys represent the industry's most significant push toward eliminating passwords entirely, backed by Apple, Google, and Microsoft through the FIDO Alliance.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Rapid adoption since 2023. Apple enabled passkeys in iOS 16/macOS Ventura, Google in Android 14/Chrome, Microsoft in Windows 11 23H2. Over 12 billion accounts are passkey-capable as of 2025. Consumer adoption is accelerating with major services (Google, Amazon, GitHub, PayPal) supporting passkeys. Enterprise adoption is slower due to MDM and conditional access integration requirements.
use_cases: Consumer authentication for web and mobile applications
Enterprise passwordless login replacing passwords and legacy MFA
Phishing-resistant authentication for high-value accounts
Cross-device authentication through QR code proximity flow
Replacing SMS OTP for account recovery and step-up authentication
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Passwords, SMS OTP, TOTP authenticator apps, legacy push-based MFA

vendor_support.apple: Full support in iCloud Keychain, Safari, iOS 16+, macOS Ventura+
vendor_support.google: Full support in Google Password Manager, Chrome, Android 14+
vendor_support.microsoft: Support in Windows Hello, Edge, with Entra ID conditional access integration
vendor_support.okta: Passkey support in Okta Identity Engine with device-bound option for enterprises
vendor_support.auth0: Passkey support through Actions pipeline with progressive enrollment

emerging_technologies.name: Platform Authenticators (Windows Hello, Touch ID, Face ID)
emerging_technologies.description: Platform authenticators are biometric or PIN-based authenticators built into the user's device operating system. Windows Hello uses facial recognition, fingerprint, or PIN backed by TPM 2.0. Apple Touch ID and Face ID use the Secure Enclave for cryptographic operations. These authenticators provide device-bound FIDO2 credentials that do not sync across devices (unlike passkeys), offering stronger assurance that the user is on a specific managed device. Platform authenticators are invisible to users once enrolled, providing a seamless authentication experience. They satisfy AAL2 requirements and, when hardware-backed, can satisfy AAL3 for verifier impersonation resistance.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Widely deployed across consumer and enterprise environments. Windows Hello for Business is standard in enterprise Windows deployments with over 150 million monthly active users. Apple biometrics are ubiquitous on iOS and macOS devices. Platform authenticators are the foundation for both device-bound credentials and synced passkeys. Most modern laptops and smartphones ship with hardware-backed biometric authenticators.
use_cases: Enterprise Windows Hello for Business login replacing passwords
macOS and iOS Touch ID / Face ID for application authentication
Device-bound strong authentication for regulated environments requiring non-exportable keys
Seamless MFA that does not require a separate hardware token
Authentication for privileged access where device identity binding is critical
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Password-only authentication, hardware OTP tokens, smart cards (in some enterprise scenarios)

vendor_support.microsoft: Windows Hello for Business with TPM 2.0, Entra ID integration, WHFB cloud trust or hybrid key trust
vendor_support.apple: Touch ID on MacBook, Face ID on iPhone/iPad, integrated with Keychain and enterprise MDM
vendor_support.google: Android biometric authentication with Keystore, Chrome platform authenticator support
vendor_support.okta: FastPass agent leverages platform authenticators for device-bound passwordless
vendor_support.azure-entra: Native support for Windows Hello for Business as primary authentication method

emerging_technologies.name: Hardware Security Keys (YubiKey, Titan, Feitian)
emerging_technologies.description: Hardware security keys are physical FIDO2/U2F devices that provide the highest level of phishing-resistant authentication. They generate and store cryptographic key pairs in tamper-resistant hardware, with private keys that can never be extracted. Authentication requires physical presence (touch or tap) and optionally a PIN or biometric (on keys with fingerprint readers like YubiKey Bio). Security keys provide verifier impersonation resistance through origin binding, making them immune to adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing attacks. They support multiple protocols (FIDO2, U2F, PIV, OATH) and work across platforms without drivers. Security keys are the gold standard for privileged access and are mandated by some regulatory frameworks for administrative accounts.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Established in enterprise security for privileged users. Google eliminated phishing attacks among 85,000+ employees using Titan keys. Widely adopted in financial services, government, and technology sectors for admin accounts. Consumer adoption remains limited due to cost ($25-70 per key) and the need for backup keys. Increasingly required by cyber insurance policies for administrative access. YubiKey 5 series and Google Titan are market leaders.
use_cases: Privileged access authentication for system administrators and security teams
Phishing-resistant MFA for high-value enterprise accounts
Passwordless login for cloud admin consoles (Azure, AWS, GCP)
Government and regulated industry authentication meeting AAL3
SSH key-based authentication using FIDO2 resident keys
Backup authentication method for passkey recovery scenarios
emerging_technologies.migration_from: RSA SecurID tokens, smart cards, TOTP authenticator apps, SMS OTP

vendor_support.yubico: YubiKey 5 series with FIDO2, PIV, OATH; YubiKey Bio for fingerprint; YubiEnterprise subscription for fleet management
vendor_support.google: Titan Security Key (USB-A, USB-C, NFC); integrated with Google Workspace and GCP
vendor_support.microsoft: Full support in Entra ID passwordless authentication, Windows Hello security key sign-in
vendor_support.feitian: ePass FIDO2 keys with biometric options; cost-effective for large deployments
vendor_support.thales: SafeNet FIDO2 keys for enterprise with remote management capabilities

emerging_technologies.name: Passwordless Phone Sign-In
emerging_technologies.description: Passwordless phone sign-in replaces passwords with push notifications to a registered authenticator app on the user's mobile device. When a user attempts to sign in, a notification is sent to their phone where they verify their identity using biometrics or device PIN before approving the authentication. Modern implementations include number matching (the user must enter a number displayed on the login screen into their phone) to prevent MFA fatigue attacks where attackers repeatedly send push notifications hoping the user accidentally approves one. The phone becomes the primary authenticator, combining something you have (the registered device) with something you are (biometric) or something you know (PIN). This approach offers significantly better usability than passwords while providing MFA by default.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Widely deployed in enterprise environments. Microsoft Authenticator passwordless sign-in is enabled for millions of Entra ID users. Okta FastPass provides similar functionality. Google Prompt is available for all Google accounts. Number matching has become standard after MFA fatigue attacks (e.g., the 2022 Uber breach). Push-based authentication is the most common MFA method in enterprise, though FIDO2 is preferred for highest-risk accounts due to phishing resistance limitations of push.
use_cases: Enterprise passwordless login for knowledge workers
Step-up authentication for sensitive operations
Self-service password reset replacement
Multi-device sign-in confirmation
Consumer authentication for banking and financial services
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Passwords, SMS OTP, voice call verification, email-based OTP

vendor_support.microsoft: Microsoft Authenticator with number matching, GPS-based location context, and Entra ID conditional access
vendor_support.okta: Okta Verify with FastPass for passwordless; push with number challenge
vendor_support.duo: Duo Push with Verified Duo (number matching); Duo Mobile passwordless
vendor_support.google: Google Prompt for all Google accounts; enterprise via Google Workspace
vendor_support.ping: PingID mobile app with swipe, biometric, and number matching authentication

emerging_technologies.name: Modern Certificate-Based Authentication
emerging_technologies.description: Modern certificate-based authentication (CBA) uses X.509 digital certificates stored on devices or hardware tokens for passwordless authentication. Unlike legacy smart card deployments, modern CBA integrates with cloud identity providers and supports mobile devices. The user's private key is stored in the device TPM, secure enclave, or on a PIV-compatible smart card, and authentication is performed through TLS client certificate presentation or FIDO2 attestation. Modern CBA systems integrate with cloud PKI services for automated certificate lifecycle management including issuance, renewal, and revocation through SCEP or EST protocols. CBA provides strong non-repudiation and is particularly valued in government, defense, and healthcare sectors where regulatory requirements mandate certificate-based identity assurance.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Transitioning from legacy on-premises PKI to cloud-managed certificates. Microsoft Entra CBA enables cloud-native certificate authentication without ADFS. US federal agencies use PIV/CAC cards mandated by HSPD-12. Healthcare organizations use certificates for EHR access. Adoption is increasing as cloud PKI solutions (Smallstep, EJBCA SaaS, Venafi) reduce operational complexity. Certificate-based machine identity (mTLS) is growing rapidly in zero trust architectures.
use_cases: US government PIV/CAC card authentication for federal systems
Healthcare professional authentication for EHR and clinical systems
High-assurance enterprise authentication meeting AAL3 with hardware-backed keys
Mutual TLS for service-to-service authentication in zero trust architectures
Smart manufacturing and IoT device certificate-based identity
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Username/password with smart card add-on, legacy ADFS certificate authentication, on-premises RADIUS/PKI

vendor_support.microsoft: Entra ID CBA supporting X.509 certificates from any CA, integrated with conditional access policies
vendor_support.yubico: YubiKey PIV module for certificate storage; YubiHSM for CA key protection
vendor_support.venafi: Cloud-based PKI with automated certificate lifecycle management across enterprise
vendor_support.smallstep: Open-source and commercial CA with ACME, SCEP, EST; device attestation integration
vendor_support.keyfactor: Enterprise PKI platform with EJBCA and certificate lifecycle management

emerging_technologies.name: Email Magic Link Authentication
emerging_technologies.description: Magic link authentication sends a time-limited, single-use URL to the user's registered email address. Clicking the link authenticates the user without requiring a password. The link contains a cryptographic token that is validated by the server, and the token typically expires within 10-15 minutes. Magic links delegate security to the email account, making the email provider the de facto identity provider. This approach is popular for consumer applications where friction-free onboarding is prioritized over high-assurance authentication. However, magic links are not phishing-resistant (the link can be forwarded) and rely on email security. They are classified as a restricted authenticator by NIST SP 800-63B because email is a single factor that can be compromised through email account takeover.
standards: No formal standard; implementations are proprietary
OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) used by some implementations
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Widely used in consumer SaaS applications (Slack, Notion, Medium, Substack). Popular for low-friction onboarding where email verification serves as both identity proofing and authentication. Being phased out by some services in favor of passkeys. Not recommended for enterprise or high-assurance applications due to reliance on email security and lack of phishing resistance. Some implementations enhance security by requiring the link to be opened on the same device or browser that initiated the login.
use_cases: Consumer SaaS application login with zero-friction onboarding
Account recovery as an alternative to security questions
Invitation-based access for collaboration tools
Low-risk application authentication where password management overhead is unwarranted
Initial authentication for progressive enrollment into stronger authenticators

vendor_support.auth0: Passwordless connection type with customizable magic link emails and token lifetimes
vendor_support.stytch: Magic link API with device fingerprinting and rate limiting; primary product offering
vendor_support.firebase: Email link sign-in through Firebase Authentication with custom domain support
vendor_support.supabase: Magic link authentication built into Supabase Auth with PKCE flow
vendor_support.clerk: Email magic link as a primary authentication method with session management

emerging_technologies.name: Continuous Biometric Authentication
emerging_technologies.description: Continuous biometric authentication moves beyond point-in-time identity verification to ongoing confirmation that the authenticated user remains present and in control throughout the session. Technologies include continuous facial presence detection (ensuring the authenticated face remains in front of the camera), behavioral biometrics (analyzing typing patterns, mouse movements, and touch screen pressure), gait analysis for mobile devices, and ambient audio fingerprinting. Machine learning models build a behavioral profile for each user and calculate a real-time confidence score that can trigger step-up authentication or session termination when anomalies are detected. This approach aligns with zero trust continuous verification principles. Privacy implications are significant, requiring careful implementation with user consent and data minimization.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Early-stage deployment primarily in high-security environments. Financial services institutions use behavioral biometrics for fraud detection during online banking sessions. Government and defense organizations pilot continuous facial recognition for classified workstations. Major vendors (BioCatch, BehavioSec/LexisNexis) provide behavioral biometric layers that overlay existing authentication. Consumer adoption is limited by privacy concerns and computational overhead. GDPR and BIPA regulations create compliance requirements for biometric data processing.
use_cases: Fraud detection in online banking and financial trading platforms
Classified workstation access with continuous presence verification
Insider threat detection through behavioral deviation analysis
Call center agent identity verification during customer interactions
High-value transaction authorization with continuous user presence
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Session-based authentication with fixed timeouts, periodic re-authentication prompts

vendor_support.biocatch: Behavioral biometrics platform analyzing cognitive and physiological patterns during sessions
vendor_support.lexisnexis-behaviosec: Behavioral biometrics integrated into LexisNexis Digital Identity Network
vendor_support.microsoft: Continuous Access Evaluation in Entra ID uses risk signals but not biometrics directly
vendor_support.aware: Knomi mobile biometric framework for continuous face and voice verification
vendor_support.callsign: Intelligence-driven authentication combining device, location, and behavioral biometrics

emerging_technologies.name: Phishing-Resistant OTP Alternatives
emerging_technologies.description: Phishing-resistant OTP alternatives are authentication mechanisms designed to replace traditional TOTP and SMS OTP with methods that provide verifier impersonation resistance. These include transaction-bound OTPs where the code is cryptographically tied to the specific transaction being authorized (not just the login session), QR-code-initiated FIDO2 cross-device flows where a code initiates a proximity-based cryptographic handshake, and smart card PIN-protected OTP generators that verify the relying party identity before displaying a code. These approaches address the fundamental weakness of traditional OTPs: they can be phished because the user types the code into whatever site they are on, with no verification that the site is legitimate. Transaction binding ensures the code is only valid for the specific operation it was generated for.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Limited deployment, primarily in financial services and payments. 3-D Secure 2.0 provides transaction-bound authentication for card-not-present payments. FIDO2 hybrid transport (QR code to phone) is gaining traction as a phishing-resistant alternative to SMS OTP. Banking regulations (PSD2 SCA in Europe) drive adoption of transaction-bound authentication. Most organizations are bypassing this category entirely by moving directly from legacy OTP to passkeys or FIDO2 security keys.
use_cases: High-value financial transaction authorization with amount and recipient binding
Payment authentication under PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication requirements
Cross-device authentication where FIDO2 hybrid transport replaces SMS OTP
Legacy system authentication where FIDO2 direct integration is not yet possible
Regulatory compliance where transaction-specific authentication is mandated
emerging_technologies.migration_from: SMS OTP, TOTP authenticator apps, email OTP, voice call verification

vendor_support.yubico: YubiKey OATH module with OCRA challenge-response for transaction binding
vendor_support.onespan: Digipass hardware and software authenticators with transaction data signing (CRONTO)
vendor_support.thales: SafeNet trusted access with transaction-bound OTP capabilities
vendor_support.entersekt: Mobile-first transaction signing and authentication for financial services
vendor_support.fido-alliance: CTAP2 hybrid transport specification enabling QR-code-initiated cross-device FIDO2 flows
